content:
# Luke Chapter 12 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 102-104 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_012_page_001.jpg, LUK_012_page_002.jpg, LUK_012_page_003.jpg

---

**1** In the meantime, when the myriads of the crowd had gathered together, so that they trampled upon one another, he began to say to his disciples first, "Beware of the leaven of the Pharisees, which is hypocrisy.

**2** But there is nothing covered up that shall not be revealed, and hidden that shall not be made known.

**3** Therefore whatsoever things you have said in the darkness shall be heard in the light, and what you have spoken to the ear in the inner chambers shall be proclaimed upon the housetops.

**4** And I say to you, my friends, do not fear those who kill the body and after this have nothing more that they can do.

**5** But I will show you whom you should fear: fear the one who, after killing, has authority to cast into Gehenna. Yes, I say to you, fear this one.

**6** Are not five sparrows sold for two assaria? And not one of them is forgotten before God.

**7** But even the hairs of your head are all numbered. Do not fear; you are of more value than many sparrows.

**8** And I say to you, everyone who confesses me before men, the Son of Man also shall confess him before the angels of God.

**9** But the one who denies me before men shall be denied before the angels of God.

**10** And everyone who shall speak a word against the Son of Man, it shall be forgiven him; but to the one who blasphemes against the Holy Spirit, it shall not be forgiven.

**11** And when they bring you before the synagogues and the rulers and the authorities, do not be anxious how or what you shall answer or what you shall say;

**12** for the Holy Spirit shall teach you in that very hour what it is needful to say.

**13** And someone from the crowd said to him, "Teacher, tell my brother to divide the inheritance with me."

**14** But he said to him, "Man, who appointed me a judge or a divider over you?"

**15** And he said to them, "Take heed and guard yourselves from all covetousness, for not in the abundance of one's possessions does his life consist."

**16** And he spoke a parable to them, saying, "The land of a certain rich man brought forth plentifully.

**17** And he was reasoning within himself, saying, 'What shall I do, for I have no place where I shall store my crops?'

**18** And he said, 'This I will do: I will tear down my barns and build greater ones, and I will store there all my grain and my goods.

**19** And I will say to my soul, "Soul, you have many goods laid up for many years; rest, eat, drink, be merry."'

**20** But God said to him, 'Fool! This night your soul is required of you; and the things you have prepared, whose shall they be?'

**21** So is the one who stores up treasure for himself and is not rich toward God."

**22** And he said to his disciples, "Therefore I say to you, do not be anxious for your soul, what you shall eat, nor for your body, what you shall put on.

**23** For the soul is more than food, and the body more than clothing.

**24** Consider the ravens, that they neither sow nor reap, which have neither storehouse nor barn, and God feeds them. How much more are you of value than the birds!

**25** And which of you by being anxious is able to add a cubit to his stature?

**26** If therefore you are not able to do even the least thing, why are you anxious about the rest?

**27** Consider the lilies, how they grow: they neither toil nor spin; yet I say to you, not even Solomon in all his glory was arrayed as one of these.

**28** But if God so clothes the grass in the field, which today exists and tomorrow is cast into the oven, how much more shall he clothe you, O you of little faith!

**29** And you, do not seek what you shall eat and what you shall drink, and do not be of anxious mind.

**30** For all these things the nations of the world seek after; but your Father knows that you have need of these things.

**31** But seek his kingdom, and these things shall be added to you.

**32** Do not fear, little flock, for it was your Father's good pleasure to give you the kingdom.

**33** Sell your possessions and give alms. Make for yourselves purses that do not grow old, an unfailing treasure in the heavens, where no thief comes near nor moth destroys.

**34** For where your treasure is, there also shall your heart be.

**35** Let your loins be girded and your lamps burning,

**36** and you yourselves be like men who are waiting for their lord, when he shall return from the wedding feast, that when he comes and knocks they may open to him immediately.

**37** Blessed are those servants whom the lord, when he comes, shall find watching. Truly I say to you, he shall gird himself and have them recline at table, and having come he shall serve them.

**38** And if he shall come in the second watch, and if in the third watch, and find them so, blessed are those servants.

**39** But know this, that if the master of the house had known at what hour the thief was coming, he would not have allowed his house to be broken into.

**40** You also, be ready, for at an hour you do not expect, the Son of Man comes."

**41** And Peter said, "Lord, do you speak this parable to us, or also to all?"

**42** And the Lord said, "Who then is the faithful steward, the wise one, whom the lord shall set over his household servants, to give them their portion of food at the proper time?

**43** Blessed is that servant whom his lord, when he comes, shall find so doing.

**44** Truly I say to you, he shall set him over all his possessions.

**45** But if that servant should say in his heart, 'My lord delays in coming,' and should begin to beat the menservants and the maidservants, and to eat and drink and be drunk,

**46** the lord of that servant shall come on a day when he does not expect and at an hour which he does not know, and shall cut him asunder and appoint his portion with the unfaithful.

**47** And that servant who knew the will of his lord, and did not prepare nor do according to his will, shall be beaten with many stripes.

**48** But the one who did not know, yet did things worthy of stripes, shall be beaten with few. And to everyone to whom much was given, much shall be required of him; and to whom they entrusted much, of him they shall ask the more.

**49** I came to cast fire upon the earth, and how I wish it were already kindled!

**50** But I have a baptism to be baptized with, and how I am constrained until it is accomplished!

**51** Do you think that I came to give peace on the earth? No, I say to you, but rather division.

**52** For from now on there shall be five in one house divided, three against two and two against three.

**53** They shall be divided, father against son and son against father, mother against daughter and daughter against mother, mother-in-law against her daughter-in-law and daughter-in-law against mother-in-law."

**54** And he said also to the crowds, "When you see a cloud rising from the west, immediately you say, 'A rainstorm is coming,' and so it happens.

**55** And when a south wind is blowing, you say, 'There will be scorching heat,' and it happens.

**56** Hypocrites! You know how to discern the face of the earth and of the heaven, but how is it you do not know how to discern this present time?

**57** And why do you not even of yourselves judge what is right?

**58** For as you are going with your adversary before a magistrate, on the way give diligence to be released from him, lest he drag you to the judge, and the judge deliver you to the officer, and the officer cast you into prison.

**59** I say to you, you shall by no means come out from there until you have paid the very last lepton."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus)

1. **12:1 — "myriads of the crowd" (ton myriadon tou ochlou):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΩΝ ΜΥΡΙΑΔΩΝ ΤΟΥ ΟΧΛΟΥ, using the genitive absolute construction. The term ΜΥΡΙΑΔΩΝ ("myriads, tens of thousands") is a Lukan hyperbole indicating an immense gathering. This is rendered formally rather than paraphrasing as "thousands." The scene follows directly upon the escalating hostility of 11:53-54 and marks a shift to public discourse.

2. **12:1 — "leaven" (zyme) as hypocrisy:** Sinaiticus reads ΗΤΙΣ ΕΣΤΙΝ ΥΠΟΚΡΙΣΙΣ ("which is hypocrisy"), explicitly identifying the leaven metaphor. This interpretive gloss is unique to Luke's version; Mark 8:15 and Matthew 16:6 leave the leaven unidentified. The relative pronoun ΗΤΙΣ (qualitative "which is of such a nature") suggests an essential identification rather than a mere comparison.

3. **12:5 — "Gehenna" (Geennan):** Sinaiticus reads ΓΕΕΝΝΑΝ, the Greek transliteration of the Hebrew GE-HINNOM ("Valley of Hinnom"). This is retained untranslated as a proper theological term rather than rendering it "hell," preserving the specific eschatological geography of the Greek text. This is the only occurrence of Gehenna in Luke's Gospel.

4. **12:6 — "two assaria" (assarion duo):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΣΣΑΡΙΩΝ ΔΥΟ. The assarion was a small Roman copper coin worth approximately one-sixteenth of a denarius. Matthew 10:29 records two sparrows for one assarion; Luke's version gives five sparrows for two assaria — an even cheaper rate per bird, heightening the argument from lesser to greater regarding God's care.

5. **12:8-9 — Confession and denial pattern:** Sinaiticus preserves the chiastic structure: confessing/Son of Man confesses (v. 8) and denying/shall be denied (v. 9). Notably, verse 8 uses the active "the Son of Man shall confess him" while verse 9 uses the divine passive "shall be denied" (ΑΠΑΡΝΗΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ), leaving the agent unstated. This asymmetry — explicit agency in blessing, veiled agency in judgment — is preserved in the formal translation.

6. **12:10 — The unforgivable blasphemy:** Sinaiticus reads ΤΩ ΕΙΣ ΤΟ ΑΓΙΟΝ ΠΝΕΥΜΑ ΒΛΑΣΦΗΜΗΣΑΝΤΙ ΟΥΚ ΑΦΕΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ ("to the one who blasphemes against the Holy Spirit, it shall not be forgiven"). The distinction between speaking against the Son of Man (forgivable) and blaspheming the Holy Spirit (unforgivable) stands as one of the most solemn declarations in the Gospel tradition. The formal translation preserves the grammatical distinction between ΕΙΠΩΝ ("speaks a word") and ΒΛΑΣΦΗΜΗΣΑΝΤΙ ("blasphemes").

7. **12:14 — "Man" (Anthrope):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΝΘΡΩΠΕ, the vocative of direct address. Jesus's refusal to adjudicate an inheritance dispute ("who appointed me a judge or divider") is significant for Lukan theology: the kingdom transcends property disputes. The vocative "Man" is rendered formally rather than softening to a modern equivalent.

8. **12:15 — "covetousness" (pleonexias):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΛΕΟΝΕΞΙΑΣ ("covetousness, greediness, desire for more"). The compound word (PLEON + ECHEIN, "to have more") denotes not merely desire but the insatiable drive to possess beyond one's need. Rendered "covetousness" as the formal theological equivalent, connecting to the Decalogue prohibition (Exod 20:17 LXX).

9. **12:19 — "Soul" (Psyche):** Sinaiticus reads ΨΥΧΗ throughout this passage (vv. 19-20, 22-23). The rich fool addresses his own PSYCHE — the seat of life, desire, and self — with materialist consolation. The same word appears in God's devastating reply: "your PSYCHE is required of you." The formal equivalent "soul" is maintained throughout to preserve this verbal and theological connection.

10. **12:20 — "your soul is required of you" (ten psychen sou apaitousin apo sou):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΠΑΙΤΟΥΣΙΝ ("they require"), using the third person plural as a circumlocution for divine action — a Semitic idiom avoiding direct attribution to God. The verb ΑΠΑΙΤΕΩ means "to demand back, to require repayment," implying that the soul was on loan. This is rendered formally: "your soul is required of you."

11. **12:25 — "cubit to his stature" (epi ten helikian autou pechyn):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΗΧΥΝ ΕΠΙ ΤΗΝ ΗΛΙΚΙΑΝ ΑΥΤΟΥ. The word ΗΛΙΚΙΑ can mean either "stature" (height) or "span of life" (age). ΠΗΧΥΣ ("cubit," approximately 18 inches) more naturally suits physical height, but adding a cubit to one's lifespan would indeed be a "least thing" (v. 26) compared to adding it to one's height. The ambiguity is preserved with "stature," the more literal rendering.

12. **12:27 — "the lilies" (ta krina):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΑ ΚΡΙΝΑ, typically identified as the crown anemone (Anemone coronaria) common in the Galilean landscape, though any number of wildflowers may be intended. The traditional rendering "lilies" is maintained. The Sinaiticus text reads ΟΥ ΚΟΠΙΑ ΟΥΔΕ ΝΗΘΕΙ ("they neither toil nor spin"), the standard reading.

13. **12:29 — "do not be of anxious mind" (me meteorizesthe):** Sinaiticus reads ΜΗ ΜΕΤΕΩΡΙΖΕΣΘΕ, a rare word in the New Testament (hapax legomenon). The verb ΜΕΤΕΩΡΙΖΩ literally means "to raise up, to suspend in mid-air," and metaphorically "to be in anxious suspense, to be tossed about." Rendered "do not be of anxious mind" to capture the sense of unsettled, suspended worry. Some translations render this "do not worry," but the Greek is more vivid than the common ΜΕΡΙΜΝΑΩ used elsewhere in the passage.

14. **12:32 — "little flock" (to mikron poimnion):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΟ ΜΙΚΡΟΝ ΠΟΙΜΝΙΟΝ. This diminutive pastoral image — unique to Luke — addresses the disciples as a small, vulnerable flock. The verb ΕΥΔΟΚΗΣΕΝ ("was well pleased, took delight") is aorist, indicating a decisive divine act: the gift of the kingdom is not a future promise but a settled determination of the Father. This verse is among the most concentrated theological statements in Luke's Gospel.

15. **12:33 — "purses that do not grow old" (ballantia me palaoumena):** Sinaiticus reads ΒΑΛΛΑΝΤΙΑ ΜΗ ΠΑΛΑΙΟΥΜΕΝΑ. The word ΒΑΛΛΑΝΤΙΟΝ ("purse, money bag") appears in Luke 10:4 and 22:35-36. The participle ΠΑΛΑΙΟΥΜΕΝΑ ("growing old, wearing out") creates a paradox: earthly purses decay, but heavenly purses do not. The metaphor extends to "unfailing treasure" (ΘΗΣΑΥΡΟΝ ΑΝΕΚΛΕΙΠΤΟΝ), a term found only here in the New Testament.

16. **12:35 — "Let your loins be girded" (estosan hymon hai osphyes periezosmenai):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΣΤΩΣΑΝ ΥΜΩΝ ΑΙ ΟΣΦΥΕΣ ΠΕΡΙΕΖΩΣΜΕΝΑΙ, a periphrastic perfect imperative construction. The imagery of girded loins recalls the Passover preparation (Exod 12:11) and the readiness of servants for action. Combined with burning lamps, this creates a vivid picture of alert, expectant service.

17. **12:37 — The lord serves his servants:** Sinaiticus reads ΠΕΡΙΖΩΣΕΤΑΙ ΚΑΙ ΑΝΑΚΛΙΝΕΙ ΑΥΤΟΥΣ ΚΑΙ ΠΑΡΕΛΘΩΝ ΔΙΑΚΟΝΗΣΕΙ ΑΥΤΟΙΣ ("he shall gird himself and have them recline and having come shall serve them"). This astonishing reversal — the master serving his watchful servants — is unique to Luke and foreshadows Jesus's declaration at the Last Supper (22:27): "I am among you as the one who serves." The formal translation preserves each verb in sequence.

18. **12:38 — "second watch... third watch":** Sinaiticus reads ΕΝ ΤΗ ΔΕΥΤΕΡΑ ΦΥΛΑΚΗ ΚΑΙ ΕΝ ΤΗ ΤΡΙΤΗ ΦΥΛΑΚΗ. This reflects the Jewish division of the night into three watches (as opposed to the Roman four-watch system used in Mark 13:35). The second watch ran approximately from 10 PM to 2 AM, and the third from 2 AM to 6 AM — the deepest hours of night, emphasizing the difficulty and merit of sustained watchfulness.

19. **12:42 — "faithful steward, the wise one" (pistos oikonomos ho phronimos):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΙΣΤΟΣ ΟΙΚΟΝΟΜΟΣ Ο ΦΡΟΝΙΜΟΣ. The ΟΙΚΟΝΟΜΟΣ ("steward, household manager") was a trusted slave responsible for provisioning the entire household. The article before ΦΡΟΝΙΜΟΣ ("the wise one") is distinctive, setting off the quality as an appositive: "the faithful steward, the wise one." Matthew 24:45 reads "faithful and wise servant" (ΔΟΥΛΟΣ). Luke's substitution of OIKONOMOS for DOULOS shifts the emphasis from general servitude to specific managerial responsibility.

20. **12:46 — "cut him asunder" (dichotomesei auton):** Sinaiticus reads ΔΙΧΟΤΟΜΗΣΕΙ ΑΥΤΟΝ, literally "shall cut him in two." This severe verb (ΔΙΧΟΤΟΜΕΩ, from DICHA + TEMNO, "to cut apart") may be literal or metaphorical for utter destruction. Rendered "cut him asunder" to preserve the formal severity of the Greek. The punishment is then compounded: his "portion" (ΜΕΡΟΣ) is assigned "with the unfaithful" (ΜΕΤΑ ΤΩΝ ΑΠΙΣΤΩΝ) — Luke's term where Matthew has "with the hypocrites."

21. **12:49 — "fire upon the earth" (pyr epi ten gen):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΥΡ ΗΛΘΟΝ ΒΑΛΕΙΝ ΕΠΙ ΤΗΝ ΓΗΝ ("I came to cast fire upon the earth"). The nature of this fire is debated: purifying judgment, the Holy Spirit (cf. Acts 2:3), or the divisive crisis that Jesus's mission provokes. The exclamation ΤΙ ΘΕΛΩ ΕΙ ΗΔΗ ΑΝΗΦΘΗ ("how I wish it were already kindled") expresses intense eschatological urgency. The formal translation preserves the ambiguity without interpretive reduction.

22. **12:50 — "baptism" (baptisma):** Sinaiticus reads ΒΑΠΤΙΣΜΑ ΔΕ ΕΧΩ ΒΑΠΤΙΣΘΗΝΑΙ ("I have a baptism to be baptized with"). The metaphorical use of ΒΑΠΤΙΣΜΑ for suffering and death connects to Mark 10:38-39. The verb ΣΥΝΕΧΟΜΑΙ ("I am constrained, pressed, held together") describes the anguished tension of anticipation. This is one of the most revealing statements of Jesus's inner experience in the Synoptic tradition.

23. **12:51 — "division" (diamerismon):** Sinaiticus reads ΔΙΑΜΕΡΙΣΜΟΝ ("division, distribution") where Matthew 10:34 has ΜΑΧΑΙΡΑΝ ("a sword"). Luke's term is more precise: the result of Jesus's coming is not violence per se but the fracturing of the most intimate human bonds. The ΔΙΑΜΕΡΙΣΜΟΣ is then illustrated concretely in vv. 52-53 with the household divisions.

24. **12:53 — Family divisions:** Sinaiticus reads the list of divided family relationships following Micah 7:6 (LXX). The structure is carefully arranged in pairs: father/son, mother/daughter, mother-in-law/daughter-in-law. Each pair is stated twice (A against B, B against A), emphasizing the mutual and reciprocal nature of the division. This allusion to Micah places the present crisis within the prophetic expectation of the eschatological day.

25. **12:56 — "Hypocrites" (Hypokritai):** Sinaiticus reads ΥΠΟΚΡΙΤΑΙ, connecting back to the "hypocrisy" (ΥΠΟΚΡΙΣΙΣ) of verse 1. The chapter thus forms an inclusio: it opens with warning against the leaven of hypocrisy and closes with the charge of hypocrisy directed at those who can read weather signs but not the signs of the times. The word ΚΑΙΡΟΝ ("time, season, appointed moment") in verse 56 carries eschatological weight.

26. **12:58 — Legal terminology:** Sinaiticus employs precise legal vocabulary: ΑΝΤΙΔΙΚΟΣ ("adversary, legal opponent"), ΑΡΧΩΝ ("magistrate, ruler"), ΚΡΙΤΗΣ ("judge"), ΠΡΑΚΤΩΡ ("officer, bailiff, tax-collector"). The ΠΡΑΚΤΩΡ is a distinctly Lukan term (appearing only here in the NT) referring to an officer responsible for exacting debts or enforcing sentences. Each term is rendered with its formal legal equivalent.

27. **12:59 — "the very last lepton" (ton eschaton lepton):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΟΝ ΕΣΧΑΤΟΝ ΛΕΠΤΟΝ. The ΛΕΠΤΟΝ was the smallest copper coin in circulation, worth half a quadrans (Mark 12:42). Matthew 5:26 uses ΚΟΔΡΑΝΤΗΣ ("quadrans"); Luke characteristically uses the smaller denomination, emphasizing the thoroughness of the accounting. The transliteration "lepton" is retained as the formal equivalent.

### Translation Philosophy

1. **Formal equivalence in eschatological discourse:** Luke 12 is dominated by eschatological teaching — watchfulness, judgment, the coming crisis. The formal translation preserves the urgency and directness of the Greek imperatives (ΦΟΒΗΘΗΤΕ, "fear"; ΦΥΛΑΣΣΕΣΘΕ, "guard yourselves"; ΓΙΝΕΣΘΕ ΕΤΟΙΜΟΙ, "be ready") without softening their force into contemporary idiom.

2. **Parabolic integrity maintained:** The Parable of the Rich Fool (vv. 16-21), the watchful servants (vv. 35-40), and the faithful steward (vv. 42-48) are rendered with careful attention to the narrative structure and rhetorical force of the Greek. Direct speech within parables is preserved at each level of embedding, including God's direct address to the fool (v. 20) and the fool's soliloquy to his own soul (v. 19).

3. **Monetary and measurement terms:** Technical terms (ΑΣΣΑΡΙΟΝ, ΛΕΠΤΟΝ, ΠΗΧΥΣ) are either transliterated or rendered with their standard English equivalents rather than converted to modern currency or measurements, preserving the first-century context and allowing the reader to encounter the text on its own terms.

4. **Theological vocabulary consistency:** Key terms maintained from previous chapters: kingdom of God, Son of Man, the Holy Spirit, soul (PSYCHE), faith/faithful (PISTIS/PISTOS). The term OIKONOMOS ("steward") is introduced here and will recur in Luke 16:1-8, establishing important thematic continuity.

5. **Sinaiticus priority throughout:** The Sinaiticus text is followed consistently. Notable readings include the three-watch Jewish system (v. 38) rather than the four-watch Roman system, "the unfaithful" (APISTOI) rather than "the hypocrites" at v. 46, and "division" (DIAMERISMON) rather than "a sword" at v. 51.

6. **Structural coherence of the chapter:** Luke 12 forms a carefully structured discourse moving from warnings about hypocrisy (vv. 1-3), through fear and confession (vv. 4-12), covetousness and anxiety (vv. 13-34), watchfulness and stewardship (vv. 35-48), to the crisis of division and discernment (vv. 49-59). The translation preserves the connecting particles (DE, OUN, GAR) that mark these transitions, maintaining the logical flow of the argument.

7. **Consistency with Luke 11:** The translation maintains continuity with the preceding chapter, particularly the escalating conflict with the Pharisees (11:53-54 leading to 12:1), the theme of divine provision (11:9-13 anticipating 12:22-31), and the Christological self-designation "Son of Man" used in both judgment (11:30) and eschatological promise (12:8, 40).
